buggy_code:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('multi_asset_returns.csv', parse_dates=['date'])
df = df.sort_values(['asset', 'date'])

# Shift WITHOUT groupby — last row of asset A bleeds into first row of asset B
df['prev_return'] = df['returns'].shift(1)

# Use as feature
df['signal'] = (df['prev_return'] > 0).astype(int)
clean_code:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('multi_asset_returns.csv', parse_dates=['date'])
df = df.sort_values(['asset', 'date'])

# Shift WITHIN each asset group
df['prev_return'] = df.groupby('asset')['returns'].shift(1)

df['signal'] = (df['prev_return'] > 0).astype(int)
description: shift(1) applied to multi-asset DataFrame without groupby — last row of one asset bleeds into first row of next.
explanation: When data is sorted by asset then date, df['returns'].shift(1) shifts across the asset boundary. The first return of MSFT (row N) gets the last return of AAPL (row N-1) as its lagged value. This is cross-asset data contamination — MSFT's signal uses AAPL's prior return.
suggested_fix: Always use groupby('asset') when shifting in multi-asset DataFrames: df.groupby('asset')['returns'].shift(1)

issues.title: shift() without groupby contaminates data across asset boundaries
